name: Vía de la Plata and Vía Verde del Oeste Loop
name_original: Puente de hierro sobre el río Esla desde Benavente — circular del Camino Natural Vía de la Plata
description: Hike the Vía de la Plata and Vía Verde del Oeste Loop, a moderate 6.9-mile route with historical sights and river views near Benavente.
region_intro: Hiking around Benavente, Spain, offers diverse trails through a landscape characterized by its riverine paths, lagoons, and historical routes. Situated in the province of Zamora, the region features accessible paths along the Esla, Eria, and Órbigo rivers. The area is also known for its natural reserves, such as the Lagoons of Vilafáfila, which provide opportunities for birdwatching and gentle walks. These natural features create varied terrain suitable for different hiking experiences. Best hiking trails around Benavente The most popular hiking route is Basilia's Balcony and Coto, a moderate 4.9 miles (7.8 km) trail that takes 2 hours 13 minutes to complete. This route offers varied terrain and scenic views. Another top favourite among local hikers is Vía de la Plata and Vía Verde del Oeste Loop, a moderate 6.9 miles (11.1 km) path. This trail combines historical pilgrimage routes with greenway sections, offering a mix of cultural and natural scenery. Local hikers also love the El Semillero Lagoons Loop, a 4.3 miles (6.9 km) trail leading through a natural lagoon area, often completed in about 1 hour 48 minutes. Hiking around Benavente is defined by its riverine paths, historical Vías Verdes, and natural lagoons. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and challenging hikes, catering to various ability levels. The routes in Benavente are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from 4 reviews. More than 60 hikers have used komoot to explore Benavente's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are there around Benavente?
A: Benavente offers a good selection of hiking opportunities, with over 25 trails available for exploration. These routes cater to various preferences, including easy and moderate options.

Q: Are there easy hiking trails in Benavente?
A: Yes, Benavente has 10 easy hiking trails, perfect for a relaxed walk or for those new to hiking. These routes often follow riverine paths and offer gentle terrain.

Q: Are there challenging hikes around Benavente?
A: The hiking trails around Benavente are primarily categorized as easy or moderate. There are no trails currently listed as difficult, making the area suitable for a wide range of fitness levels without extreme challenges.

Q: Are Benavente's hiking trails suitable for families?
A: Many of Benavente's trails, especially the easy and moderate ones along the rivers and through natural lagoon areas, are well-suited for families. The gentle gradients and scenic views make for an enjoyable outing with children.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the hiking trails in Benavente?
A: Generally, dogs are welcome on many of the natural trails around Benavente, especially those in open areas like riverbanks and rural paths. However, it's always advisable to keep them on a leash and check for any specific local regulations, particularly in protected natural areas like the Lagoons of Vilafáfila.

Q: Are there circular hiking routes available?
A: Yes, Benavente offers several circular hiking routes. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1305470296/el-semillero-lagoons-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Semillero Lagoons Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you through a natural lagoon area. Another is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1305317182/via-de-la-plata-and-via-verde-del-oeste-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía de la Plata and Vía Verde del Oeste Loop&lt;/a&gt;, combining historical paths with greenway sections.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while hiking?
A: While hiking around Benavente, you can explore diverse landscapes including riverine paths along the Esla, Eria, and Órbigo rivers. You might also encounter historical bridges like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5422592" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bridge of the Vía Verde de la Plata&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2810181" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bridge over the Esla River&lt;/a&gt;. The area is also known for its natural lagoons, such as those explored on the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1305470296/el-semillero-lagoons-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Semillero Lagoons Loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Benavente?
A: The hiking routes in Benavente are highly regarded by the komoot community, holding an average rating of 4.5 stars. Hikers often appreciate the varied terrain, scenic river views, and the blend of natural and historical elements found along the paths.

Q: What is the best time of year for hiking in Benavente?
A: Spring and autumn are generally the most pleasant seasons for hiking in Benavente, offering mild temperatures and vibrant natural scenery. Summer can be warm, especially in open areas, so early mornings or late afternoons are recommended if hiking during this period. Winter hiking is also possible, with riverine paths remaining accessible.

Q: Is parking available near the trailheads?
A: Parking availability varies by trailhead. Many routes that start from or pass through towns and villages like Benavente itself or Barcial del Barco will have public parking options nearby. For more remote starting points, it's advisable to check specific route details or local maps for designated parking areas.

Q: How can I reach the hiking trails using public transport?
A: Benavente is a regional hub, and some trails are accessible from the town center. For routes starting further afield, local bus services might connect to nearby villages. However, public transport options can be limited for reaching all trailheads directly, so planning ahead or considering a car is often recommended for maximum flexibility.
tips: In the historic center, the Church of Santa María del Azogue, from the 16th century, and in Gothic style, stands out as the main monument of Benavente.
Plaza Mayor de Benavente that includes buildings such as the Benavente Town Hall and the Information and Tourism Office, banks, cafes and bars. Everything you may need on your trip.
Bridge converted from a disused railway line that crosses the Esla River and connects this incredible natural area along the Vía de la Plata Natural Trail.
A renovated castle and gardens rising above the public gardens below. The Parador hotel is one of the most visited monuments in Benavente.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this loop?
faqs.answer: This loop combines sections that likely feature characteristics of both the Vía de la Plata and Vías Verdes. You can expect generally flat paths, often on well-packed dirt or gravel, as is typical for greenways. Some parts might involve walking on wider red paths or tractor tracks, and potentially some asphalt through towns. The overall elevation change is minimal, making for a gentle walking experience.

faqs.question: How long does it take to hike this loop?
faqs.answer: The loop is approximately 11.1 kilometers (7 miles) long and typically takes around 2 hours and 53 minutes to complete, depending on your pace and stops.

faqs.question: What can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: The loop offers a mix of historical and scenic points of interest. You'll pass by the impressive Church of Santa María del Azogue and the central Plaza Mayor de Benavente. A notable feature is the Iron Bridge Over the Esla River, and you'll also get a View of Castillo de Benavente.

faqs.question: Is the Vía de la Plata and Vía Verde del Oeste Loop suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: Yes, with its moderate difficulty, gentle gradients, and relatively short distance of 11.1 km, this loop is well-suited for beginners and families. The terrain is generally easy, making it accessible for most fitness levels.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this loop?
faqs.answer: Spring and autumn are generally the most pleasant seasons for hiking in this region, offering mild temperatures. Summer can be very hot, especially in the plains, which might make the hike more challenging. Winter can be cooler but also offers a unique, quieter experience.

faqs.question: Is this loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the Vía de la Plata and Vías Verdes are generally dog-friendly, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially when passing through towns, near livestock, or in areas with wildlife. Ensure you carry enough water for your dog, particularly on warmer days.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or is there an entrance fee for this trail?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need a permit, and there are no entrance fees to hike this loop. It is freely accessible to the public.

faqs.question: Where does the trail start and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The loop is circular, meaning you can start at various points. Given the highlights like the Plaza Mayor de Benavente, starting in Benavente would be a logical choice. Parking should be available in or around the town center, though specific trailhead parking is not detailed.

faqs.question: Does this loop intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route incorporates sections of several other paths. You will find it overlaps with parts of the Mtb Vía de la Plata, Vía de la Plata hiking - 03 Salamanca to Astorga, and the Vía verde del oeste. Smaller portions also coincide with the Camino de Santiago del Sureste and local paths like Arrabal Cadañeras Pago and Camino del Borreguil.

faqs.question: Are there cafes or restaurants along the route?
faqs.answer: Given that the loop passes through Benavente and its Plaza Mayor, you will find various cafes, restaurants, and shops within the town to refuel or relax. However, sections outside of urban areas may have limited facilities, so it's wise to carry snacks and water.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife or plants can I expect to see?
faqs.answer: Along the route, especially in the greener sections, you might encounter typical Spanish countryside flora such as holm oaks, cork trees, and olive groves. Wildlife sightings could include various bird species, and small mammals common to agricultural and natural areas.
